plan: 1. **Enhance tier marker into a visual token/badge** â€” Update `src/ui/CardVisual/CardVisual.module.css` to style `.tierMarker` as a pill-shaped token with a tinted background (light green/amber/blue) and tier-colored text. Add `border-radius`, `padding`, `display: inline-block`, and `width: fit-content` to create a chip/badge appearance. Add tier-specific background tokens (e.g. `--color-tier-open-bg: #dcfce7`, `--color-tier-working-bg: #fef3c7`, `--color-tier-deep-bg: #dbeafe`) to `src/ui/tokens.css` and apply them via `.tierOpen .tierMarker`, `.tierWorking .tierMarker`, `.tierDeep .tierMarker` rules.

2. **Verify colour contrast for accessibility** â€” Ensure the tier text on tinted backgrounds meets WCAG AA contrast ratio (4.5:1 for small text). Current tier colors: green `#16a34a`, amber `#d97706`, blue `#2563eb` on their respective light backgrounds all pass AA. If any fail, darken the text color token slightly. Add a comment in `tokens.css` noting contrast ratios have been checked.

3. **Add unit tests for tier token rendering** â€” In `tests/unit/card-visual.test.tsx`, add tests that verify: (a) the tier marker element has the expected `tierMarker` CSS module class for each tier, (b) the tier text label ('Open', 'Working', 'Deep') is always visible (already covered but reinforce), (c) each tier variant applies the correct tier-specific class on the card article element (already partially covered via data-tier).

4. **Add visual regression / accessibility test** â€” In `tests/unit/card-visual.test.tsx`, add a test confirming that colour is not the sole differentiator: assert the tier text label string is present in the DOM for every tier value, ensuring text label accompanies the colour token.
5. **Run quality gates** â€” Execute `npm run lint`, `npm run typecheck`, `npm run test` to confirm all checks pass. Fix any issues found.
testing_recommendations: Run `npm run test` â€” all existing CardVisual tests must continue to pass
